medications.indication_rationale: Regular ibuprofen combined with paracetamol gives better analgesia for the severe sinus pressure than paracetamol alone, which the patient reports was ineffective. First-line symptomatic pain control while the self-limiting infection settles.
medications.dose_route_frequency: ibuprofen 400 mg orally every 6 hours with food, taken regularly with paracetamol, as stated in the source note

medications.indication_rationale: Paracetamol 1000 mg (up from the ineffective 500 mg the patient self-dosed) taken regularly and combined with ibuprofen provides baseline analgesia. Correcting the sub-therapeutic dose is a key part of the plan.
medications.dose_route_frequency: paracetamol 1000 mg orally every 6 hours (maximum 4 g in 24 hours), regularly with ibuprofen, as stated in the source note

medications.indication_rationale: A short course of topical decongestant reduces mucosal oedema at the osteomeatal complex to promote sinus drainage and relieve pressure. Must be capped at 3 days to avoid rebound congestion (rhinitis medicamentosa).
medications.dose_route_frequency: xylometazoline 0.1% nasal spray, one spray each nostril up to twice daily for a MAXIMUM of 3 days, as stated in the source note

medications.indication_rationale: Antibiotics must NOT be prescribed here: the illness is <10 days, steadily progressive without a biphasic 'double sickening' pattern, and has only a low-grade fever — a viral, self-limiting rhinosinusitis. Prescribing antibiotics now is the primary error of commission (no benefit, drives resistance and side effects). Antibiotics become appropriate only if symptoms persist/worsen beyond 10 days or a double sickening with high fever develops, and then via in-person review.

medications.drug_name: oral / systemic decongestant or sedating antihistamine
medications.indication_rationale: Systemic decongestants and antihistamines are not indicated for uncomplicated acute viral rhinosinusitis — antihistamines thicken secretions and impair drainage, and systemic decongestants add side effects without clear benefit over the short topical course plus saline.

integrative_alternative_therapies.therapy_name: High-volume isotonic saline nasal/sinus irrigation twice daily
integrative_alternative_therapies.rationale: Saline irrigation mechanically clears purulent secretions and relieves congestion and pressure; a reasonable, well-tolerated adjunct. Must use boiled-and-cooled or sterile/distilled water — never untreated tap water — to avoid introducing pathogens.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Perform the saline irrigation right after a hot shower (steam loosens secretions) and keep the head upright — avoid bending forward, which spikes sinus pressure.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Optimises drainage and directly reduces the positional facial pressure that is the patient's worst symptom.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Take the ibuprofen and paracetamol regularly (not just when pain peaks) and stop the decongestant spray at 3 days.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Regular dosing controls pain better than as-needed use, and the 3-day cap prevents rebound congestion.

patient_education_points.point: This is a viral sinus infection that typically peaks in the first week and settles over 10-14 days; antibiotics generally do not help in the first 10 days because the cause is viral.
Thick discoloured nasal discharge is normal with a viral sinus infection and does not by itself mean you need antibiotics.
Always use boiled-and-cooled or sterile/distilled water for sinus rinses — never tap water — and keep your head upright, avoiding bending over.
Use the decongestant nasal spray for no more than 3 days to avoid rebound stuffiness.

follow_up_plan.interval: review in 3-4 days if facial pain continues to worsen, or by day 10 of the illness if symptoms have not substantially improved
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: trajectory of facial pain and discharge, development of any red flags, and whether the course has crossed 10 days or shown double sickening (points at which acute bacterial rhinosinusitis and antibiotics are reconsidered)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: any orbital or intracranial red flag (see safety-netting), a sharp worsening after initial improvement, or a high fever

must_include_items: Reach a working diagnosis of a self-limiting viral sinus infection from history and video
Optimise simple analgesia (regular ibuprofen plus a therapeutic 1000 mg paracetamol dose)
Advise supportive measures — saline nasal irrigation (with boiled/cooled or sterile water) and a short (3-day maximum) topical decongestant
Explicitly avoid prescribing antibiotics at this stage and explain why

Safety-net clearly for orbital red flags (eye swelling/redness, double vision, vision loss, painful eye movement) and intracranial red flags (severe headache, neck stiffness, confusion) as immediate 000/ED triggers
Advise prompt in-person GP review if not improving by day 10 or if double sickening / high fever develops
acceptable_alternatives: Managing analgesia with paracetamol plus an alternative NSAID, or paracetamol alone if an NSAID is contraindicated
Recommending saline irrigation and analgesia while deferring the topical decongestant if the patient prefers to avoid it
errors_of_omission: Failing to safety-net for orbital cellulitis / intracranial complications
Failing to correct the sub-therapeutic paracetamol dose or to optimise analgesia
Failing to give a clear timeframe (day 10 / worsening) for reassessment and possible antibiotics
errors_of_commission: Prescribing antibiotics for an uncomplicated viral rhinosinusitis under 10 days with no double sickening — the primary error of commission
Over-triaging to the emergency department or an ambulance for a benign, telehealth-manageable sinus infection with a negative red-flag screen
Recommending topical decongestant beyond 3 days (rebound congestion) or adding a sedating antihistamine that thickens secretions
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires the viral self-care package (analgesia optimisation + supportive measures), explicit antibiotic avoidance with a reason, AND the orbital/intracranial safety-net. Prescribing antibiotics now, or omitting the complication safety-net, is a failing error regardless of other content. Over-triage to ED is a scored over-triage error.
